verses.text: God sends Saul to carry out a long-deferred judgment on Amalek, who had ambushed Israel in the wilderness. The command is total. God's justice is patient but sure; what He purposes against persistent evil He will, in His own time, certainly perform.

God sends Saul to carry out a long-deferred judgment on Amalek, who had ambushed Israel in the wilderness. The command is total. God's justice is patient but sure; what He purposes against persistent evil He will, in His own time, certainly perform.

God sends Saul to carry out a long-deferred judgment on Amalek, who had ambushed Israel in the wilderness. The command is total. God's justice is patient but sure; what He purposes against persistent evil He will, in His own time, certainly perform.

Saul gathers a great army and first warns the Kenites to leave Amalek, sparing the kind for an old kindness once shown to Israel. Even on a mission of judgment, mercy distinguishes the innocent. God does not sweep away the good together with the guilty.

Saul gathers a great army and first warns the Kenites to leave Amalek, sparing the kind for an old kindness once shown to Israel. Even on a mission of judgment, mercy distinguishes the innocent. God does not sweep away the good together with the guilty.

Saul gathers a great army and first warns the Kenites to leave Amalek, sparing the kind for an old kindness once shown to Israel. Even on a mission of judgment, mercy distinguishes the innocent. God does not sweep away the good together with the guilty.

Saul strikes Amalek, but spares king Agag and 'the best of the sheep,' destroying only what was worthless. This is obedience on his own terms, keeping the parts he likes. Half-obedience that saves what God said to give up is really disobedience dressed up.

Saul strikes Amalek, but spares king Agag and 'the best of the sheep,' destroying only what was worthless. This is obedience on his own terms, keeping the parts he likes. Half-obedience that saves what God said to give up is really disobedience dressed up.

Saul strikes Amalek, but spares king Agag and 'the best of the sheep,' destroying only what was worthless. This is obedience on his own terms, keeping the parts he likes. Half-obedience that saves what God said to give up is really disobedience dressed up.

'It repenteth me that I have set up Saul to be king,' says the LORD, 'for he is turned back from following me.' God speaks of grief in human terms — not that He changes, but that sin truly grieves Him. Our turning back is no small thing to God.

'It repenteth me that I have set up Saul to be king,' says the LORD, 'for he is turned back from following me.' God speaks of grief in human terms — not that He changes, but that sin truly grieves Him. Our turning back is no small thing to God.

Samuel finds Saul, who greets him, 'I have performed the commandment of the LORD.' But the bleating of the spared sheep betrays him. Saul cannot hear his own sin; the very evidence of it is in his ears, yet he claims he obeyed. Sin makes us deaf to ourselves.

Samuel finds Saul, who greets him, 'I have performed the commandment of the LORD.' But the bleating of the spared sheep betrays him. Saul cannot hear his own sin; the very evidence of it is in his ears, yet he claims he obeyed. Sin makes us deaf to ourselves.

Samuel cuts through it: when Saul was 'little in thine own sight,' God made him king and sent him on a clear errand — 'wherefore then didst thou not obey?' The root of the failure was a heart no longer small before God. Pride is the soil where disobedience grows.

Samuel cuts through it: when Saul was 'little in thine own sight,' God made him king and sent him on a clear errand — 'wherefore then didst thou not obey?' The root of the failure was a heart no longer small before God. Pride is the soil where disobedience grows.

Samuel cuts through it: when Saul was 'little in thine own sight,' God made him king and sent him on a clear errand — 'wherefore then didst thou not obey?' The root of the failure was a heart no longer small before God. Pride is the soil where disobedience grows.

Saul protests again: he did obey, and the people kept the best 'to sacrifice unto the LORD.' He drapes plain disobedience in religious language. To disobey God and then offer Him what we withheld is to insult Him while pretending to worship Him.

Saul protests again: he did obey, and the people kept the best 'to sacrifice unto the LORD.' He drapes plain disobedience in religious language. To disobey God and then offer Him what we withheld is to insult Him while pretending to worship Him.

Samuel answers with words that ring through Scripture: 'to obey is better than sacrifice... rebellion is as the sin of witchcraft.' God wants our obedience, not gifts offered in its place. To set our will against His word is rebellion, however religious it may look.

Samuel answers with words that ring through Scripture: 'to obey is better than sacrifice... rebellion is as the sin of witchcraft.' God wants our obedience, not gifts offered in its place. To set our will against His word is rebellion, however religious it may look.

Saul finally says, 'I have sinned' — but adds, 'I feared the people, and obeyed their voice.' His confession blames others, and his real fear is human, not divine. Samuel pronounces it: 'thou hast rejected the word of the LORD, and the LORD hath rejected thee.'

Saul finally says, 'I have sinned' — but adds, 'I feared the people, and obeyed their voice.' His confession blames others, and his real fear is human, not divine. Samuel pronounces it: 'thou hast rejected the word of the LORD, and the LORD hath rejected thee.'

Saul finally says, 'I have sinned' — but adds, 'I feared the people, and obeyed their voice.' His confession blames others, and his real fear is human, not divine. Samuel pronounces it: 'thou hast rejected the word of the LORD, and the LORD hath rejected thee.'

As Saul clutches Samuel's robe, it tears, and Samuel says the kingdom is likewise torn from him and given to a better man. 'The Strength of Israel will not lie nor repent.' God's final word on a hardened heart stands; His verdicts are not bluffs to be coaxed away.

As Saul clutches Samuel's robe, it tears, and Samuel says the kingdom is likewise torn from him and given to a better man. 'The Strength of Israel will not lie nor repent.' God's final word on a hardened heart stands; His verdicts are not bluffs to be coaxed away.

As Saul clutches Samuel's robe, it tears, and Samuel says the kingdom is likewise torn from him and given to a better man. 'The Strength of Israel will not lie nor repent.' God's final word on a hardened heart stands; His verdicts are not bluffs to be coaxed away.

Saul's chief concern even now is to be honoured before the elders, and Samuel goes with him to keep up appearances of worship. A heart that has lost God's favour often clings hardest to people's esteem. Reputation becomes the idol when reality with God is gone.

Saul's chief concern even now is to be honoured before the elders, and Samuel goes with him to keep up appearances of worship. A heart that has lost God's favour often clings hardest to people's esteem. Reputation becomes the idol when reality with God is gone.

Samuel himself hews Agag in pieces, finishing the judgment Saul left undone. The work God assigns must be fully done; what disobedience leaves half-finished, faithfulness completes. Mercy misplaced toward evil is not kindness but compromise.

Samuel himself hews Agag in pieces, finishing the judgment Saul left undone. The work God assigns must be fully done; what disobedience leaves half-finished, faithfulness completes. Mercy misplaced toward evil is not kindness but compromise.

Samuel mourns for Saul to the end, and 'the LORD repented that he had made Saul king.' Even God's righteous judgments are grieved over, not gloated upon. To watch a soul ruin itself by disobedience is cause for tears, even when the sentence is just.
